summary:
The Hittite Kingdom emerged in central Anatolia during the seventeenth century BC, when a series of warrior kings consolidated control over the Anatolian plateau and established their capital at [[event:1818|Hattusa]]. The Hittites were an Indo-European-speaking people who absorbed earlier Hattic and Hurrian cultural traditions, creating a distinctive civilization that blended Anatolian, Mesopotamian, and steppe influences.

At its height, the Hittite Empire was one of the great powers of the ancient Near East, competing with Egypt and Mitanni for dominance over Syria and the Levant. The empire reached its greatest territorial extent under kings such as Suppiluliuma I (r. c. 1344–1322 BC), who dismantled the [[event:1831|Mitanni Kingdom]] and installed Hittite-allied rulers across northern Syria. This expansion brought the Hittites into direct conflict with New Kingdom Egypt, culminating in the [[event:1808|Battle of Kadesh]] around 1274 BC.

Hittite governance was characterized by an elaborate treaty system through which the king regulated relationships with vassal states and foreign powers. The [[event:1882|Hittite Laws]] represent one of the earliest surviving legal codes, notable for emphasizing restitution over corporal punishment. The Hittites also pioneered [[event:1857|iron smelting]] techniques in the eastern Mediterranean, giving them a potential military and economic advantage.

The Hittite Empire collapsed dramatically around 1180–1178 BC during the broader [[event:1815|Bronze Age Collapse]], destroyed by a combination of the [[event:1814|Sea Peoples]], internal rebellion, drought, and famine. The capital Hattusa was burned and abandoned. Successor Neo-Hittite city-states survived in Syria for several more centuries, preserving elements of Hittite culture and language before being absorbed by the [[event:61|Assyrian Empire]].
